stem: Why does increasing receiver bandwidth reduce SNR?

options.text: It prevents hydrogen from resonating
It eliminates all transverse magnetization before readout
It reduces the number of protons in the selected slice
A wider frequency range admits more random noise

explanation: Noise is distributed across frequency, so a wider receiver window samples more noise power. Signal does not rise proportionally, causing SNR to decline.

stem: If receiver bandwidth is reduced by a factor of two and other parameters are unchanged, how does SNR change approximately?

options.text: It increases by the square root of two
It doubles
It decreases by the square root of two
It remains exactly unchanged

explanation: SNR is approximately inversely proportional to the square root of receiver bandwidth. Halving bandwidth therefore raises SNR by about 1.41 rather than by a factor of two.

stem: Which artifact is commonly reduced by increasing receiver bandwidth?

options.text: T1 saturation from a short TR
Frequency-direction chemical shift
Phase wrap caused solely by a small phase FOV
Incomplete fat inversion in STIR

explanation: Higher bandwidth maps a given fat-water frequency difference across fewer pixels, reducing type 1 chemical shift displacement. It can also reduce susceptibility distortion, at the cost of SNR.

stem: Which combination is most likely after lowering receiver bandwidth?

options.text: Lower SNR with less chemical shift
Higher SNR with a shorter dwell time
Higher SNR with greater chemical shift and susceptibility-related distortion
No SNR change with complete artifact removal

explanation: Lower bandwidth samples less noise and improves SNR, but its longer dwell time magnifies off-resonance displacement and distortion. Bandwidth selection therefore balances signal against artifact behavior.

stem: What acquisition change accompanies an increase in receiver bandwidth?

options.text: Slower sampling with a longer dwell time
Fewer hydrogen nuclei in each voxel
Mandatory use of a longer TR
Faster sampling with a shorter readout interval

explanation: Wide bandwidth requires rapid sampling and short dwell time. This shortens the readout window and can reduce off-resonance distortion.
